sections.title: Prior to A Tail of Two Cats

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Hello. What's your name?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: I shouldn't talk to strangers.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Well, my name is [player name] and I'm pleased to meet you. So, now that I'm not a stranger anymore, what's your name?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Hmm... Nice to meet you, [player name]. I'm Neite.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How come you're the only cat around here?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Oh there's far more than just me. The others are just shy. We do also get visitors from outside the city sometimes, like that Bob. He's quite a handsome beast...

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Oh, I see. Romance is in the air, is it?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Well, life here in the desert can be a lonely existence, even with all my busy work... Still, to be honest, he worries me.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: In what way?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Well, I pride myself on my knowledge of the family lines of cats. Obviously, Bob features quite heavily in most family trees, but he himself has none!

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What do you mean?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Nobody is sure where he came from. Not even the Sphinx knows. It's just one of those mysteries.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Interesting...

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What are you doing?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: I'm hunting frogs and locusts.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Why? Do they taste good?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Yuck! No, I would never debase myself to eat such filth.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I see you're a cat of taste then.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: I am a cat of high breeding! My family tree dates back before those of any ruler on Gielinor.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What do you do with the frogs and locusts?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: What do you mean by 'do'?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Well, seeing as you're hunting them, I thought you'd eat them too.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: No, no, I just hunt them, play with them for a bit and then kill them.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Isn't that a little evil?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: What's evil about a little destruction?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What isn't? You destroy and take away life. What good is there in that?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Life can come from destruction too. Take a volcano. It will destroy all life that surrounds it, but it will also fertilise and enrich the surrounding earth.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: From that earth, more life will spring forth than there was in the first place.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: How do you compare killing frogs to a volcano? A volcano has no choice about whether it explodes or not, but you do have a choice.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: I disagree. It's in my nature to prey on smaller life forms in the same way as it is in the volcanoes nature to explode.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Don't they sometimes call the Devourer the goddess of destruction? You seem to have a lot in common with a goddess that your kind oppose.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: That is not too surprising. A long time ago, I served her. Her form has changed a lot since then, just as mine has.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: You mean you weren't always a cat?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: No, I used to be human. At the time when Amascut became the Devourer, I was one of her chief priestesses.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So you stopped serving her because of that change?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Not initially. Many of us tolerated her new habits for some time. She went too far though, so we took action.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What did you do?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: The last of her priestesses gathered and cursed her. Unfortunately, she also cast a spell of her own, condemning us to an eternity of walking Gielinor in her form.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So she's a cat then?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: She's as much of a cat as Icthlarin is a dog.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: What curse did you cast upon her?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: We trapped her in a human form, limiting her power. We also cursed her to never be willing worshipped again, though I fear that curse has started to wane.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So that's why she's scared of cats?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Indeed. There are fewer of us priestesses left now, but we still hold enough power to be quite the problem for her.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Do you talk to the Sphinx much?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Not really. She is normally too wrapped up in preserving her immortality to be making conversation.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So you don't like her then?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Nothing of the sorts. I have nothing but respect for her.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Why is that?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Well, because her immortality is based on the fact that she keeps us cats safe. Her whole existence is dedicated to protecting us.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: That doesn't seem like much of a life.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: True. Life shouldn't be hoarded, but lived. Of course, don't tell her I said that, otherwise I'd have to be on the lookout for a new guardian.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Tell me about you and the Devourer.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Before she was the goddess of destruction, she was Amascut, the goddess of rebirth. Back then, I was human, and I served as one of her chief priestesses.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: When she became the Devourer, many of us tolerated her new habits for some time. After all, destruction was key to being the goddess of rebirth, so it was something we were comfortable with.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: But she went too far?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Yes, so we took action. The last of her priestesses gathered and cursed her. Unfortunately, she also cast a spell of her own, condemning us to an eternity of walking Gielinor in her form.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Our curse trapped her in a human form, limiting her power. We also cursed her to never be willing worshipped again, though I fear that curse has started to wane.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So that's why she's scared of cats?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Indeed. There are fewer of us priestesses left now, but we still hold enough power to be quite the problem for her.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I'll leave you to it.

sections.title: After A Tail of Two Cats

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Hello again, Neite.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Hello.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Where's Bob?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Out gallivanting. I think he's hunting noobs in the Wilderness this time. You know what he's like.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So, why aren't you out there with him?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: I like to roam as much as the next cat, but, every so often, I do like to come home.

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: So, you and Bob haven't had an argument?

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Who told you that?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: Er... nobody.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: He's meant to be with you. You haven't lost him have you?

lines.speaker: Player
lines.text: I don't think so.

lines.speaker: Neite
lines.text: Well keep an eye on him for me.
